questions.question: By default, how is all data in a Fabric warehouse encrypted at rest?

options.A: It is not encrypted unless customer-managed keys are configured
options.B: Using customer-managed keys stored in Azure Key Vault
options.C: Using Microsoft-managed keys
options.D: Using keys generated by the workspace Admin

questions.explanation: All warehouse data is encrypted at rest by default using Microsoft-managed keys. For greater control you can configure customer-managed keys (CMK) through Azure Key Vault.

questions.question: Which permission must a non-admin user be granted to view unmasked data on a table protected by dynamic data masking?

options.A: ALTER ANY MASK
options.B: CONTROL
options.C: UNMASK
options.D: SELECT

questions.explanation: To allow a specific non-admin user to see unmasked values, grant them the UNMASK permission. This gives fine-grained control over who sees real data without granting full admin or table ownership rights.

questions.question: Which operations are affected by an RLS filter predicate?

options.A: SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE
options.B: INSERT only
options.C: SELECT and INSERT only
options.D: All DML operations including INSERT

questions.explanation: Rows where the filter predicate returns false are invisible for SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations. INSERT is not affected by filter predicates.

questions.question: When a user has both a GRANT and a DENY on the same permission from different roles, which one takes effect?

options.A: GRANT always wins
options.B: DENY always wins
options.C: The most recently applied statement wins
options.D: Neither applies and access is undefined

questions.explanation: When a user has both a GRANT and a DENY on the same permission, DENY always wins. This is why CLS works by granting broad table access and then denying access to sensitive columns.

questions.question: According to the principle of least privilege, what permission should an application that reads data only through stored procedures be given?

options.A: SELECT on the underlying tables
options.B: CONTROL on the warehouse
options.C: EXECUTE on those stored procedures, not SELECT on the tables
options.D: Workspace Member role

questions.explanation: Least privilege means giving each user or application only the permissions it needs. An application that reads through stored procedures should have EXECUTE on those procedures, not SELECT on the underlying tables.

questions.question: A Fabric warehouse holds a Customers table with sensitive email and credit card columns. The team wants nonprivileged analysts to query the table for reporting but never see real email or card values, with no schema or application changes. Which approach best fits?

options.A: Row-level security with a filter predicate
options.B: Dynamic data masking on the Email and CreditCardNumber columns
options.C: Deny SELECT on the entire Customers table
options.D: Customer-managed keys in Azure Key Vault

questions.explanation: DDM obscures column values in query results without changing the underlying data, schema, or application. It is the right fit for letting analysts query the table while hiding real email and card values.

questions.question: An analyst has been granted UNMASK on dbo.Customers, but the security team now wants to stop them from seeing real values again. Which statement removes that ability?

options.A: DENY UNMASK ON dbo.Customers TO [analyst@contoso.com];
options.B: ALTER TABLE Customers ALTER COLUMN Email ADD MASKED WITH (FUNCTION = 'default()');
options.C: REVOKE UNMASK ON dbo.Customers FROM [analyst@contoso.com];
options.D: DROP MASKED ON dbo.Customers;

questions.explanation: To revoke access to unmasked data, use REVOKE UNMASK ON dbo.Customers FROM [user]. This returns the user to seeing masked output without altering the masking rules themselves.

questions.question: A salesperson reports they can see other salespeople's rows in a shared Sales table even though an RLS policy exists. They are a workspace Contributor. What is the most likely explanation?

options.A: RLS never applies to Contributors automatically
options.B: The filter predicate includes an explicit condition that grants the user full visibility, and RLS otherwise applies to all users including Contributors
options.C: DDM is overriding the RLS policy
options.D: Contributors are exempt from all data protection controls by design

questions.explanation: RLS applies its predicate to all users including Admins, Members, and Contributors. If they see all rows, the predicate must explicitly include them (for example an OR condition matching their user name). RLS does not exempt Contributors automatically.

questions.question: A healthcare warehouse needs Doctors and Nurses to read the MedicalHistory column of a Patients table, while Receptionists and Patients can use the rest of the table but not that column. Which strategy implements this?

options.A: Apply dynamic data masking to MedicalHistory for everyone
options.B: Grant SELECT on the table to all roles, then DENY SELECT on the MedicalHistory column to Receptionist and Patient roles
options.C: Create an RLS filter predicate on MedicalHistory
options.D: Grant EXECUTE on a stored procedure to Doctors only

questions.explanation: Column-level security is implemented by granting broad table SELECT to all roles, then explicitly denying SELECT on the sensitive column to roles that should not see it. Because DENY wins, Receptionist and Patient lose access to MedicalHistory only.

questions.question: A Power BI report uses Direct Lake mode against a warehouse table. After column-level security is applied to that table, the team notices query performance changes. What happened?

options.A: Security stopped being enforced, so queries slowed waiting for validation
options.B: Queries automatically fell back to Direct Query mode, where security is still enforced but performance differs from the Direct Lake baseline
options.C: The report switched to Import mode and cached stale data
options.D: Direct Lake mode disabled column-level security entirely

questions.explanation: When Power BI accesses a warehouse in Direct Lake mode and a table has column-level security applied, queries automatically fall back to Direct Query mode. Security is still enforced, but query performance differs from the Direct Lake baseline.

questions.question: A warehouse architect wants users to read data only through stored procedures and never query tables directly. Which permission pattern enforces this controlled access pattern?

options.A: Grant SELECT on the tables and EXECUTE on the procedures
options.B: Grant EXECUTE on the procedures while denying direct SELECT on the underlying tables
options.C: Grant CONTROL on the procedures to all users
options.D: Grant the Viewer workspace role to all users

questions.explanation: Granting EXECUTE on procedures while denying direct table access is an effective way to enforce a controlled data access pattern, where users interact with data only through stored procedures.

questions.question: A compliance officer worries that even with dynamic data masking, a determined analyst with query access could deduce a masked salary value. Is this concern valid, and why?

options.A: No, masking physically obscures the data in storage so it cannot be inferred
options.B: Yes, because data is not physically obscured, a user could craft a query (for example forcing a divide-by-zero only when a guessed value matches) to infer real values
options.C: No, UNMASK is required to run any query against masked columns
options.D: Yes, but only if the analyst is a workspace Admin

questions.explanation: Unprivileged users with query permissions can infer actual data because it is not physically obscured. A query that divides by a masked salary column and triggers a divide-by-zero error only when a guess matches can reveal the value. DDM should be one layer, not the only control.

questions.question: A team needs to temporarily disable an active RLS security policy named sec.SalesPolicy during a data audit, without dropping it. What is the correct action?

options.A: DROP SECURITY POLICY sec.SalesPolicy
options.B: Alter the policy to set STATE = OFF
options.C: REVOKE the filter predicate function
options.D: Grant UNMASK to the auditor

questions.explanation: A security policy can be disabled temporarily by setting STATE = OFF; setting it back to STATE = ON re-enables filtering. This avoids dropping and recreating the policy.

questions.question: Security wants to limit who can change RLS security policies in a warehouse to reduce the risk of someone disabling filtering. Which permission should be restricted to trusted users only?

options.A: ALTER ANY MASK
options.B: UNMASK
options.C: ALTER ANY SECURITY POLICY
options.D: CONTROL on every table

questions.explanation: To reduce risks, the guidance is to monitor security policy changes and restrict ALTER ANY SECURITY POLICY to trusted users only.

questions.question: An organization wants to mitigate side-channel attacks that could leak filtered row values from an RLS-protected table. Which combination of controls is recommended?

options.A: Use RLS alone but increase audit retention
options.B: Combine RLS with column-level security and dynamic data masking
options.C: Replace RLS with item permissions
options.D: Encrypt the table with customer-managed keys

questions.explanation: To reduce side-channel risks, the guidance is to combine RLS with column-level security and dynamic data masking, and to monitor security policy changes.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to share a single warehouse for downstream use with an external team without granting them broad access to the whole workspace. Which security layer is appropriate?

options.A: Workspace roles
options.B: Item permissions
options.C: Encryption
options.D: Audit logs

questions.explanation: Item permissions can be granted directly to individual warehouses, letting you share a warehouse for downstream use without granting broad workspace access.

questions.question: A bank wants reporting users to see only the last four digits of stored credit card numbers, with the leading digits replaced by a fixed pattern. Which masking type and function should be used?

options.A: Default masking with default()
options.B: Email masking with email()
options.C: Custom text masking with partial(prefix, padding, suffix)
options.D: Random masking with random(low, high)

questions.explanation: Custom text masking, defined with partial(prefix, padding, suffix), exposes a specified number of characters at the start and end with custom padding in between. It is ideal for showing the last four digits of a credit card.

questions.question: An analyst is granted SELECT on dbo.SalesReport via one role and DENY SELECT on dbo.Payroll directly. What can the analyst do?

options.A: Read both SalesReport and Payroll
options.B: Read SalesReport but not Payroll
options.C: Read Payroll but not SalesReport
options.D: Read neither table

questions.explanation: GRANT SELECT on SalesReport allows reading that object, while DENY SELECT on Payroll blocks reading it. The analyst can read SalesReport but is denied Payroll.

questions.question: A reporting role only needs to read summarized data. Following least privilege, what should it be granted?

options.A: Broad SELECT on all base tables
options.B: SELECT on specific views rather than broad table access
options.C: CONTROL on the warehouse
options.D: EXECUTE on every stored procedure

questions.explanation: A reporting role that only needs to read data should have SELECT on specific views, not broad table access. This limits the impact if a credential is compromised.

questions.question: A contractor was given temporary elevated SELECT access to complete a one-time migration. The migration is now finished. What should happen according to least privilege?

options.A: Leave the access in case the contractor returns
options.B: Convert the access to a permanent role
options.C: Revoke the temporary elevated access now that the task is complete
options.D: Apply dynamic data masking instead of revoking access

questions.explanation: Least privilege guidance states that temporary elevated access should be revoked once the task is complete, limiting the impact if the credential is later compromised.

questions.question: A workspace Admin queries an RLS-protected Sales table and finds rows are being filtered out even though they expected full visibility. What is the correct fix?

options.A: Remove the Admin from the workspace
options.B: Rewrite the filter predicate to include an explicit condition for admin-level access
options.C: Grant the Admin the UNMASK permission
options.D: Disable encryption on the table

questions.explanation: RLS applies predicates to all users including Admins. If full visibility is required, you must write the predicate to include an explicit condition for admin access (for example matching the admin user name).

questions.question: A salesperson queries a shared Sales table protected by RLS so each person sees only their own orders. From the salesperson's perspective, how do their application queries change?

options.A: They must add a WHERE clause filtering on their own name
options.B: They must call a special masked view
options.C: Their queries do not change; the engine transparently scopes results to their data
options.D: They must request UNMASK before querying

questions.explanation: RLS controls which rows a user can see automatically and transparently without changing application queries. Users query the same table directly with no awareness that rows are being filtered.

questions.question: An auditor needs evidence of who changed permissions and ran queries in a Fabric warehouse over the last quarter for a compliance report. Where can this activity be reviewed?

options.A: Through SQL audit logs, available via Microsoft Purview and PowerShell
options.B: By querying the masked columns directly
options.C: By examining the RLS security policy definition
options.D: Only by enabling customer-managed keys

questions.explanation: SQL audit logs capture logins, queries, and permission changes, and audit data is available through Microsoft Purview and PowerShell with configurable event categories and retention policies for compliance reporting.

questions.question: A data engineer must manage masking rules for several tables but should not be given admin or table ownership rights. Which single grant satisfies this?

options.A: GRANT UNMASK ON each table
options.B: GRANT ALTER ANY MASK TO the engineer
options.C: GRANT CONTROL ON the warehouse
options.D: Assign the workspace Member role

questions.explanation: GRANT ALTER ANY MASK lets a data engineer add or remove masks from columns without granting admin rights. UNMASK only allows seeing unmasked data, not managing masks.

questions.question: A warehouse Admin, Member, or Contributor queries a table that has dynamic data masking applied. What do they see, and why?

options.A: Masked values, because masking applies to everyone equally
options.B: Unmasked values, because they hold the CONTROL permission
options.C: An error, because masking blocks privileged queries
options.D: Masked values unless they are explicitly granted UNMASK

questions.explanation: Users with the CONTROL permission, which includes Admins, Members, and Contributors, always see unmasked values. Only nonprivileged users see the masked output.

questions.question: Contoso Health operates a Fabric warehouse for analytics across hospitals. The Patients table holds PatientID, Name, Address, DateOfBirth, MedicalHistory, and InsuranceCardNumber. Requirements: (1) Doctors and Nurses must read MedicalHistory; Receptionists and Patients must use the table but never see MedicalHistory. (2) Each regional analyst should see only patient rows for their own region from a shared RegionalPatients table, while a national auditor sees all rows. (3) Billing reporting users should see only the last four digits of InsuranceCardNumber. (4) An ETL application reads data exclusively through stored procedures and must not query base tables directly. (5) Security wants to limit who can disable row filtering. Which combination of controls correctly satisfies all five requirements?

options.A: Use dynamic data masking for every requirement, since it can hide rows, columns, and restrict procedures
options.B: Column-level security (GRANT table SELECT then DENY SELECT on MedicalHistory) for (1); RLS with a filter predicate and security policy that includes the auditor for (2); partial() dynamic data masking on InsuranceCardNumber for (3); GRANT EXECUTE on procedures while denying direct table SELECT for (4); restrict ALTER ANY SECURITY POLICY to trusted users for (5)
options.C: Workspace roles for (1) and (2), encryption for (3), item permissions for (4), and audit logs for (5)
options.D: Row-level security for all five requirements, since predicates can filter rows, columns, and procedure calls

questions.explanation: Each requirement maps to a distinct control: CLS (GRANT then DENY on the column) protects MedicalHistory; RLS with a filter predicate and a security policy that explicitly includes the national auditor scopes regional rows; partial() masking shows only the last four digits of the insurance card; granting EXECUTE on procedures while denying direct table SELECT enforces the procedure-only access pattern; and restricting ALTER ANY SECURITY POLICY to trusted users limits who can disable filtering. No single feature covers all five, which is why the layered combination is correct.

questions.question: Consider: ALTER TABLE Customers ALTER COLUMN PhoneNumber ADD MASKED WITH (FUNCTION = 'partial(0,"XXX-XXX-",4)'); If a nonprivileged user queries a row where PhoneNumber is 555-123-7890, what is the most likely displayed value?

options.A: XXX-XXX-XXXX
options.B: XXX-XXX-7890

questions.explanation: partial(0,"XXX-XXX-",4) exposes 0 characters at the start, applies the padding string XXX-XXX-, then exposes the last 4 characters. The last four digits 7890 remain visible, producing XXX-XXX-7890.

questions.question: A developer runs: ALTER TABLE Customers ALTER COLUMN Email ADD MASKED WITH (FUNCTION = 'email()'); Later they need to fully remove masking from the Email column so nonprivileged users see real values. Which statement does this?

options.A: ALTER TABLE Customers ALTER COLUMN Email DROP MASKED;
options.B: REVOKE UNMASK ON dbo.Customers FROM PUBLIC;
options.C: ALTER TABLE Customers ALTER COLUMN Email REMOVE MASK;
options.D: DENY MASK ON dbo.Customers(Email) TO PUBLIC;

questions.explanation: To remove a mask, use ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... DROP MASKED. Revoking UNMASK would do the opposite (re-hide data for granted users), and the other options are not valid mask-removal syntax.

questions.question:
Review this RLS predicate function:

CREATE FUNCTION sec.fn_FilterByOwner(@Owner AS NVARCHAR(50))
RETURNS TABLE WITH SCHEMABINDING
AS RETURN
  SELECT 1 AS result
  WHERE @Owner = USER_NAME() OR USER_NAME() = 'manager@contoso.com';

When bound as a filter predicate on an Orders table's Owner column, which statement is true?

options.A: All users see all rows because the function always returns 1
options.B: A user sees only rows where Owner equals their own user name, plus manager@contoso.com sees every row
options.C: Only manager@contoso.com can see any rows; everyone else sees none
options.D: INSERT operations on Orders will be blocked by this predicate

questions.explanation: The function returns a row (result 1) only when @Owner matches the querying user's USER_NAME(), or when the user is manager@contoso.com (the OR grants full visibility). Rows where the predicate returns no row are filtered out. INSERT is not affected by filter predicates.

options.A: Both columns return successfully because table-level GRANT overrides the column DENY
options.B: The query fails with a permission error because DENY on the MedicalHistory column wins
options.C: Name returns and MedicalHistory returns as NULL
options.D: MedicalHistory returns masked as XXXX

questions.explanation: Column-level security relies on DENY winning over GRANT. The Receptionist has table-level SELECT but a column-level DENY on MedicalHistory, so selecting that column raises a permission error. Selecting only allowed columns (for example just Name) would succeed.

options.A: Run usp_LoadSales successfully, but cannot directly SELECT from dbo.Sales
options.B: Neither run the procedure nor query the table, because DENY on Sales blocks the procedure too
options.C: Directly query dbo.Sales but not run the procedure
options.D: Run the procedure only if also granted CONTROL on dbo.Sales

questions.explanation: This is the controlled access pattern: granting EXECUTE on the procedure while denying direct table SELECT lets the account run the procedure but not query the table directly. Users interact with data only through stored procedures.

questions.question:
An engineer writes the RLS function without SCHEMABINDING:

CREATE FUNCTION sec.fn_Filter(@SalesPerson NVARCHAR(50))
RETURNS TABLE
AS RETURN SELECT 1 AS result WHERE @SalesPerson = USER_NAME();

Compared to the documented pattern that uses RETURNS TABLE WITH SCHEMABINDING, why is WITH SCHEMABINDING used for filter predicate functions?

options.A: It makes the function return masked values automatically
options.B: It binds the function to the schema of objects it references so they cannot be changed in a way that breaks the security predicate
options.C: It is required to grant UNMASK permission
options.D: It converts the inline function into a stored procedure

questions.explanation: The documented filter predicate uses RETURNS TABLE WITH SCHEMABINDING. Schema binding ties the function to the schema of the objects it depends on so those objects cannot be altered in a way that would break the security predicate, keeping RLS enforcement reliable.

questions.question:
Consider this security policy creation:

CREATE SECURITY POLICY sec.SalesPolicy
ADD FILTER PREDICATE sec.tvf_SecurityPredicateBySalesPerson(SalesPerson)
ON [dbo].[Sales]
WITH (STATE = ON);

What is the role of the SalesPerson argument passed to the predicate function in this statement?

options.A: It is the user name of whoever creates the policy
options.B: It maps the Sales table's SalesPerson column into the function's parameter so the predicate is evaluated per row using that column value
options.C: It grants the SalesPerson column UNMASK permission
options.D: It defines a new column added to the Sales table

questions.explanation: In the ADD FILTER PREDICATE clause, the column passed (SalesPerson) is supplied to the predicate function for each row of the bound table. The function compares that column value against USER_NAME() to decide whether the row is visible.

questions.question: A developer needs to grant a single non-admin analyst the ability to see real (unmasked) values only on the dbo.Customers table, without giving them admin rights or the ability to change masks. Which statement is correct?

options.A: GRANT ALTER ANY MASK TO [analyst@contoso.com];
options.B: GRANT UNMASK ON dbo.Customers TO [analyst@contoso.com];
options.C: GRANT CONTROL ON dbo.Customers TO [analyst@contoso.com];
options.D: ALTER TABLE Customers ALTER COLUMN Email DROP MASKED;

questions.explanation: GRANT UNMASK ON dbo.Customers TO [user] gives that specific user the ability to see unmasked values on that table without admin rights. ALTER ANY MASK would let them change masks, CONTROL grants ownership-level rights, and DROP MASKED removes masking for everyone.

questions.question: An engineer applies: ALTER TABLE Accounts ALTER COLUMN Balance ADD MASKED WITH (FUNCTION = 'random(1, 100)'); A nonprivileged user repeatedly queries the same Balance value of 5000. What behavior best reflects the random() masking function?

options.A: The user always sees 0 for the Balance column
options.B: The user sees a random number within the specified range (1 to 100) in place of the real numeric value
options.C: The user sees 5000 because random() does not mask numbers
options.D: The user sees XXXX as the masked text

questions.explanation: The Random masking type, random(low, high), replaces numeric or binary values with a random number within the specified range. Here the real value 5000 is hidden behind a random number between 1 and 100, not the actual balance.
